metadata.title: Mapping the Media Genome: An Unsupervised Machine Learning Analysis of News Framing of Direct-To-Consumer Genetic Testing Kits

abstract:
Despite their heightening popularity and potential individual and societal implications, little scientific attention has been given to the news coverage of direct-toconsumer genetic testing (DTC-GT) kits (e.g. 23andMe, AncestryDNA, and MyHeritage). To accommodate, this study leverages the Analysis of Topic Model Networks to inductively develop a comprehensive overview of how major U.S. news media framed DTC-GT kits between 2009 and 2022, and then explicates the relationship of such coverage with kit sales between 2012 and 2019. We find four frames through which the news media talk about the kits: Utility, Scientific, Political-Economic, and Commercial. Further, as kit sales increase over time, news media maintain a strong emphasis on the utility of kits in their coverage, but also increasingly promote kits through product reviews. We find that this news coverage is influenced by-but does not influence-kit sales, and discuss implications pertaining to the DTC-GT industry and to biotechnology more broadly.

Due to a confluence of scientific advancements in genetic technology (Mittos et al., 2018) and a cultural shift toward curiosity for and appreciation of one's ancestry, direct-to-consumer genetic testing (DTC-GT) kits-by the likes of CONTACT Christopher M. Dobmeier

sections.paper_id: Dobmeier et al. - 2024 - Mapping the Media Genome An Unsupervised Machine Learning Analysis of News Framing of Direct-To-Con
paragraphs: 23andMe, AncestryDNA, and MyHeritage-have become high in demand. According to a 2019 a MIT Technology Review article, as many people purchased DTC-GT in 2018 as in all previous years combined (Regalado, 2019). Not only is DTC-GT marketed to provide individuals a range of personally relevant information like biological family ties and disease risk (Horton et al., 2019), but the kits are easily accessible and convenient. The procedure is as easy as spitting into a designated testing tube and mailing it to a laboratory. Within weeks, consumers receive their genetic profile, usually without the mediation of health professionals (J. S. Roberts et al., 2017). Although DTC-GT kits have so far leveraged the ingredients for product longevity-utility, availability, and convenience (Jang, 2015)-increasing ethical and legal discussions by concerned experts, politicians, journalists, and others in the media contest their longevity (Rodriguez, 2018). Uncontested, however, is the idea that using biotechnologies like DTC-GT can have lifelong outcomes, meaning that it is particularly important to study the various influences on an individual's decision to use a DTC-GT kit.

In the realm of science innovations, news media remain a prominent means of influence on public perceptions, attitudes, and behaviors (Ophir & Jamieson, 2021). This paper attempts to map the news media's coverage of DTC-GT kits in American news media, and the interaction between such coverage and realworld DTC-GT consumer behaviors. As the purchase of a DTC-GT kit may result in life-changing consequences, we pay particular attention to discussion of ethics and risks. Finally, we discuss the theoretical and practical implications for consumers, journalists, scientists, and science communicators.

sections.header_raw: Public discussions around genetic testing's opportunities and risks
sections.header_norm: public discussions around genetic testing s opportunities and risks
paragraphs: Though scientific interest in genetics goes back to the 19th century (Durmaz et al., 2015), a renewed public interest had followed the development of accessible genetic testing kits that individuals could self-administer at relatively low cost (Apathy et al., 2018). Before this, genetic mapping cost thousands of dollars and was reserved for advanced scientific purposes, such as the discovery of specific genetic anomalies like Huntington's disease (Helgason & Stefánsson, 2022). Massive research projects like the 1990s Human Genome Project have catalyzed advancements in genomics research, increasing the breadth of genetic testing capabilities and decreasing its costs (Chial, 2008). In 2019, individuals could order a kit for as low as $100 (Horton et al., 2019).

DTC-GT provides plenty of opportunities for individuals and society at large. For instance, it can reveal one's ties to ancient populations, historical migrations (Panofsky & Donovan, 2019), and familial ties (e.g., Martin, 2019). DTC-GT kits have also helped uncover links between ancestry and susceptibility to diseases (Horton et al., 2019), including cancer (Phillips, 2016), which may contribute to a healthier lifestyle (Bollinger et al., 2013).

Similarly, some kits advertise testing for reproductivity risks, personalized diets, fitness insights like athletic abilities, child talents, relationship infidelities, and even matchmaking (Phillips, 2016). These genealogical findings are perceived by many to be life-changing (Panofsky & Donovan, 2019).

While DTC-GT kits offer a range of potential benefits, they-like other biotechnologies (e.g., Yang & Hobbs, 2020)-open the door to risks and threats. Critics have argued that the results of DTC-GT may be too complex for consumers to comprehend (Einsiedel & Geransar, 2009), which could lead to unnecessary emotional distress, or even to detrimental healthrelated decision-making (Bollinger et al., 2013). Additional worries have targeted DTC-GT kits' accuracy (Horton et al., 2019). Yet, even results that correctly inform genetic predispositions can yield psychosocial and clinical effects, including anxiety (American Academy of Pediatrics, 2013;Heshka et al., 2008), especially when results are interpreted by the individual without the mediation of health experts.

Echoing more general worries about the safety of private digital data, pundits and consumers have expressed concerns over potential infringements to genetic data privacy (Rodriguez, 2018). Nearly four in every five DTC-GT kit consumers considered data privacy issues upon purchasing (J. S. Roberts et al., 2017), suggesting that consumer privacy is still a major concern for most. In addition to worries around the ability of companies to safely secure personal genetic data and avoid breeches, people also voiced a distrust in DTC-GT companies and worries that they will intentionally share data with third parties (Critchley et al., 2015). This distrust of DTC-GT companies is perpetuated by the absence of specific standards, resulting from loose federal guidelines and vague requirements for testing laboratories (Hendricks-Sturrup & Lu, 2019).

Concerns over misinterpretation of results and data privacy have sparked debates within the scientific community over the ethicality of DTC-GT (Holland et al., 2018). As outlined by the National Center for Human Genome Research (NHGRI), genetic discrimination poses a threat to equitable health insurance and employment (2020). Despite the 2008 passing of the Genetic Information Nondiscrimination Act, which outlawed this particular form of discrimination, the public still holds concerns that their genetic makeup may someday affect their ability to obtain health insurance (even when acknowledging the technology's benefits, see Haga et al., 2013). Taken together, the public is staying abreast of both the advancements and concerns of DTC-GT. As most people do not come into direct contact with technological innovations, studies indicated that a primary source for information about the benefits and risks of products like DTC-GT remain the mainstream media (Bubela & Taylor, 2008;Cappella et al., 2007;Lynch et al., 2011).

sections.paper_id: Dobmeier et al. - 2024 - Mapping the Media Genome An Unsupervised Machine Learning Analysis of News Framing of Direct-To-Con
sections.header_raw: The news framing of genetics and DTC-GT
sections.header_norm: the news framing of genetics and dtc gt
paragraphs: The mass media play a central role in conveying new and important scientific knowledge and breakthroughs (Brechman et al., 2009) and illuminating their cultural, social, and political implications (Nisbet et al., 2003). Effective news coverage can help scientific and journalistic institutions establish and bolster public trust and credibility of science while ineffective science communication can deteriorate them (Hilgard & Jamieson, 2017;Ophir & Jamieson, 2021). In other words, how the media convey scientific information is paramount to how the public processes and applies that information (Jensen, 2008) and could influence public knowledge, perceptions, and behaviors (Brossard, 2013). Thus, understanding how the media is portraying DTC-GT may clue in patterns and details relevant to public perceptions and behaviors (Dudo et al., 2007).

A wealth of scholarship illuminates biotechnology coverage trends over time. Specifically, since the early 2000s, coverage of biotechnology has risen (Cappella et al., 2005), yet oscillated between promoting and critiquing scientific innovations (e.g., Hilgard & Jamieson, 2017)-critique often in the aftermath of key events like discoveries or scandals (Listerman, 2010). With computational methods that afford unique granularity compared to those used in prior research, and particularly using an unsupervised machine learning mixed with in-depth manual content analysis, we focus, specifically, on the media's use of journalistic frames (D'Angelo, 2017).

Framing is one of the most popular theories in communication (Matthes, 2009), as well as in other disciplines such as sociology (Gamson & Modigliani, 1989) and psychology (Kahneman & Tversky, 1984). Due to its employment across different fields, framing is often described as a fractured paradigm or theory: one that suffers from inconsistent definitions, conceptualizations, and operationalization of research methods (Entman, 1993;Matthes, 2009). We therefore focus specifically on "emphasis framing" (D'Angelo, 2017).

Emphasis framing is the journalistic practice of systematically selecting and making salient certain aspects of reality in a communication text at the expense of others (Entman, 1993). Whereas equivalency framing (e.g., gain vs. loss) is suitable for the analysis of cognitions in controlled information environments (Cacciatore et al., 2016), emphasis framing is better suited for the analysis of real-world journalism (McLeod & Shah, 2015), as frames that compose the news do not often lead to comparable, controlled linguistical equivalencies. Rather, news frames encompass broader packages, consisting of words, metaphors, arguments, and more (Walter & Ophir, 2019). We thus focus our study on emphasis framing and leave it for future studies to extend our work by looking at the use and impact of frames like gain and loss (Kahneman & Tversky, 1984) on DTC-GT-related cognitions.

Myriad studies have demonstrated the impact of framing on public opinion, attitudes and behaviors in various areas and contexts. For example, media framing of social protests as either a question of free speech, a source for public disorder (Nelson et al., 1997) or as a partisan-political affair (Ophir et al., 2023) could influence public support for the movement's causes and empathy toward the protesters. In the context of electoral races, framing candidates via issues or strategy may impact perceptions (Cappella & Jamieson, 1997) and even electoral success (Walter & Ophir, 2021). Framing could be particularly important for sensemaking around complex topics, such as science, technology, and medical choices (Ophir et al., 2021). Given DTC-GT kits' increasing popularity on one hand, and their technological and ethical complexity on the other, the NHGRI has urged for comprehensive scholarship on DTC-GT coverage (2020).

Before genetic testing was available to the everyday consumer, relevant news coverage was about the field of genomics in general. Early studies pointed to this genetics coverage being lax with critique. For example, Petersen (2001) identified the frames stories of discovery (of new genes links, mutations, or functions) and unlocking nature's secrets (as in a panacea for illness). These frames emphasized the ways scientific breakthroughs have advanced the field of genomics (Hellsten & Nerlich, 2008), often pointing to examples like Dolly the Sheep and the Human Genome Project (Marks et al., 2007). However, scholars have criticized this coverage as simplistic and sensationalized (Brechman et al., 2009), exaggerating the benefits, accuracy, and reliability of DTC-GT (Bubela & Taylor, 2008).

However, over time, coverage became more nuanced, balanced, and at times, critical. For example, Molina (2004) identified news frames that balanced progress with consequences; costs with benefits; and anxieties with scientific reasoning. Critical coverage emphasized concerns around clinical validity of genetic testing (Sturdy, 2020), privacy (Lynch et al., 2011), genetic discrimination in healthcare (Caburnay et al., 2014), and biases in insurance and employment (Murphy & Maynard, 2000). Additionally, DTC-GT, like other biotechnologies, has been framed as immoral, unnatural, and dangerous, with journalists portraying scientists as playing God, opening a Pandora's Box, and creating Frankenstein's monsters (Hellsten & Nerlich, 2008, p. 93). In extreme cases, genetic testing and modification were compared to the dystopia of Aldous Huxley's Brave New World, to Faustian bargains, and even to the Nazi Holocaust (Nisbet et al., 2003). Similarly, extreme ideologies evolving around the moral question of genetic testing included notions of eugenics and genetic determinism (Lynch et al., 2011;Sturdy, 2020). The tensions between extreme promotion and criticism of genetics could lead to public polarization around the technology.

Overall, the extant body of research on the news coverage of genetic testing has contributed to several bodies of scholarship. Benefitting science communication, the findings from the aforementioned studies help trace the contours of the changing biotechnology landscape, including its influential actors (i.e., DTC-GT companies, politicians, and journalists) and events (e.g., Dolly the Sheep, the 2013 regulatory battle between the FDA and 23andMe). Research following the direct-to-consumer wave particularly illustrates strategies of risk communication-via relevant discourses on the ethicality (Lynch et al., 2011;Zimmermann et al., 2019), morality (Hellsten & Nerlich, 2008), and usage hazards (Sturdy, 2020)-that may inform consumers' genetic testing risk perceptions. Taken together, these news framing studies help piece together an innovation's diffusion into society and its corresponding coverage. Yet, although the consumer is integral to this diffusion arc, most extant research examined DTC-GT news coverage predating the 2017 spike in DTC-GT kit popularity (Regalado, 2019), perhaps missing a crucial shift in the interaction between DTC-GT and consumers (e.g., Caburnay et al., 2014;Molina, 2004;Petersen, 2001). If "a frame makes reference to something resident in the surrounding culture" (Tewksbury & Scheufele, 2019, p. 23), then this swell in DTC-GT is a necessary cultural context in which to study DTC-GT framing. Missing in the literature is a systematic analysis of the prominence and dynamics of framing of DTC-GT over time and its potential association with real-world consumer behaviors.

Further, although the abundance of prior research lays the foundation for understanding DTC-GT framing, its reliance on deductive, predetermined and theoretically driven codebooks may have limited and biased findings (Matthes & Kohring, 2008), and would therefore benefit from a complementary inductive-deductive approaches to framing (van Gorp, 2010), especially in such a rapidly evolving context. Such a problem may be exacerbated by dynamic topics, where public concerns may change over time, as was the case with genetics. Our first research goal, therefore, is to offer a systematic analysis of news framing of DTC-GT over a decadelong period that captured the arc of DTC-GT popularity. Harnessing the power of inductive, computational frame identification and precision of a deductive, theory-driven qualitative analysis for its interpretation, we asked: RQ1: What were the frames used by news media covering DTC-GT kits? RQ2: How did the salience of frames change between 2009 and 2022?

Existing literature suggests that journalism on emerging technologies has an agenda that develops alongside the product. Since members of the public learn about emerging technologies through the media, this could also mirror a development in relevant public beliefs and attitudes. For example, as nanotechnology permeated society in the early 2000s, news coverage that once favored optimism of nanotechnology later highlighted conflict and regulatory concerns (Weaver et al., 2009). That is, as nanotechnology diffused into society, so too did related national discourse and public policy. Nanotechnology, like other high-potential yet high-risk technologies require developmental freedom to reach the "high potential" stage, but regulation tends to follow this stage, as their risks become more apparent (Mandel, 2009). This phenomenon has been found in several other contexts. For example, Chuan et al. (2019) found that, as AI innovations became more prominent in everyday life (high-potential stage), news coverage of AI became more attentive to AI policies, ethical concerns, and threats (high-risk stage). Early news coverage of DTC-GT depicts the high potentiality of DTC-GT, exemplifying stories of unambiguous scientific discovery and hope (Hellsten & Nerlich, 2008;Marks et al., 2007;Petersen, 2001). If the coverage and framing of DTC-GT follows the same pattern as other emerging technologies, it will have evolved to include more discussions of the potential risks associated with DTC-GT. Simply put, as the public becomes more familiar with emerging technologies, they also become more aware of the technologies' risks. Building on prior innovation coverage trends, and given that everyday people increasingly use DTC-GT, we hypothesize that: H1: The (a) volume of ethical concerns in and (b) negativity of news coverage will increase over time.

Correspondingly, trends can vary by the frames that constitute the topic model network. Although news stories do not lend themselves to setting up mathematically equivalent frames (Shah et al., 2009), their qualitative differences may be contextualized by the journalist's tone toward DTC-GT (e.g., promotional vs. critical). To gain a clearer understanding of the mechanics of over-time trends, we ask: RQ3: Did valence of coverage vary by media frame salience?

Given the complicated dynamics between emerging technology and social institutions, we might expect that news coverage influences not only how an individual perceives an emerging technology but also their behaviors relative to that technology. Yet a prominent criticism of emphasis framing research is that it is often too descriptive, neglecting the relationships between framing and audiences' behaviors (Borah, 2011). As such, it is important that we begin to explore the role that news media coverage might have on consumer behavior (e.g., buying DTC-GT). On one hand, consumer attitudes and behavior may be influenced by news coverage of biotechnology advancements and flaws (McCluskey et al., 2016), and landmark events (Ricci & Banterle, 2020). For example, reporting on scientific breakthroughs may increase the public's curiosity of DTC-GT technology and ultimately lead to increased DTC-GT kit sales. On the other hand, increases in consumer behavior could lead to increased attention from journalists, and even change the focus of journalistic coverage. This alternative plays into journalists' well-studied selectivity bias, by which news media tend to cover well-performing companies more frequently than low-performing companies (Nokelainen & Kanniainen, 2018). In this alternative scenario, consumer purchases drive the increase in news coverage. Both scenarios seem equally plausible and would speak to the influence of news framing on consumer behavior, but they have not yet been systematically examined. Given the lack of prior systematic research parsing the relationship between coverage and consumer behavior around DTC-GT, we ask: RQ4: What is the relationship, if any, between DTC-GT kit sales and the salience of specific DTC-GT media frames?

With computational methods that afford unique granularity compared to those used in prior research, and particularly using an unsupervised machine learning mixed with in-depth manual content analysis, we focus, specifically, on the media's use of journalistic frames (D'Angelo, 2017).

Framing is one of the most popular theories in communication (Matthes, 2009), as well as in other disciplines such as sociology (Gamson & Modigliani, 1989) and psychology (Kahneman & Tversky, 1984). Due to its employment across different fields, framing is often described as a fractured paradigm or theory: one that suffers from inconsistent definitions, conceptualizations, and operationalization of research methods (Entman, 1993;Matthes, 2009). We therefore focus specifically on "emphasis framing" (D'Angelo, 2017).

Emphasis framing is the journalistic practice of systematically selecting and making salient certain aspects of reality in a communication text at the expense of others (Entman, 1993). Whereas equivalency framing (e.g., gain vs. loss) is suitable for the analysis of cognitions in controlled information environments (Cacciatore et al., 2016), emphasis framing is better suited for the analysis of real-world journalism (McLeod & Shah, 2015), as frames that compose the news do not often lead to comparable, controlled linguistical equivalencies. Rather, news frames encompass broader packages, consisting of words, metaphors, arguments, and more (Walter & Ophir, 2019). We thus focus our study on emphasis framing and leave it for future studies to extend our work by looking at the use and impact of frames like gain and loss (Kahneman & Tversky, 1984) on DTC-GT-related cognitions.

Myriad studies have demonstrated the impact of framing on public opinion, attitudes and behaviors in various areas and contexts. For example, media framing of social protests as either a question of free speech, a source for public disorder (Nelson et al., 1997) or as a partisan-political affair (Ophir et al., 2023) could influence public support for the movement's causes and empathy toward the protesters. In the context of electoral races, framing candidates via issues or strategy may impact perceptions (Cappella & Jamieson, 1997) and even electoral success (Walter & Ophir, 2021). Framing could be particularly important for sensemaking around complex topics, such as science, technology, and medical choices (Ophir et al., 2021). Given DTC-GT kits' increasing popularity on one hand, and their technological and ethical complexity on the other, the NHGRI has urged for comprehensive scholarship on DTC-GT coverage (2020).

Before genetic testing was available to the everyday consumer, relevant news coverage was about the field of genomics in general. Early studies pointed to this genetics coverage being lax with critique. For example, Petersen (2001) identified the frames stories of discovery (of new genes links, mutations, or functions) and unlocking nature's secrets (as in a panacea for illness). These frames emphasized the ways scientific breakthroughs have advanced the field of genomics (Hellsten & Nerlich, 2008), often pointing to examples like Dolly the Sheep and the Human Genome Project (Marks et al., 2007). However, scholars have criticized this coverage as simplistic and sensationalized (Brechman et al., 2009), exaggerating the benefits, accuracy, and reliability of DTC-GT (Bubela & Taylor, 2008).

However, over time, coverage became more nuanced, balanced, and at times, critical. For example, Molina (2004) identified news frames that balanced progress with consequences; costs with benefits; and anxieties with scientific reasoning. Critical coverage emphasized concerns around clinical validity of genetic testing (Sturdy, 2020), privacy (Lynch et al., 2011), genetic discrimination in healthcare (Caburnay et al., 2014), and biases in insurance and employment (Murphy & Maynard, 2000). Additionally, DTC-GT, like other biotechnologies, has been framed as immoral, unnatural, and dangerous, with journalists portraying scientists as playing God, opening a Pandora's Box, and creating Frankenstein's monsters (Hellsten & Nerlich, 2008, p. 93). In extreme cases, genetic testing and modification were compared to the dystopia of Aldous Huxley's Brave New World, to Faustian bargains, and even to the Nazi Holocaust (Nisbet et al., 2003). Similarly, extreme ideologies evolving around the moral question of genetic testing included notions of eugenics and genetic determinism (Lynch et al., 2011;Sturdy, 2020). The tensions between extreme promotion and criticism of genetics could lead to public polarization around the technology.

Overall, the extant body of research on the news coverage of genetic testing has contributed to several bodies of scholarship. Benefitting science communication, the findings from the aforementioned studies help trace the contours of the changing biotechnology landscape, including its influential actors (i.e., DTC-GT companies, politicians, and journalists) and events (e.g., Dolly the Sheep, the 2013 regulatory battle between the FDA and 23andMe). Research following the direct-to-consumer wave particularly illustrates strategies of risk communication-via relevant discourses on the ethicality (Lynch et al., 2011;Zimmermann et al., 2019), morality (Hellsten & Nerlich, 2008), and usage hazards (Sturdy, 2020)-that may inform consumers' genetic testing risk perceptions. Taken together, these news framing studies help piece together an innovation's diffusion into society and its corresponding coverage. Yet, although the consumer is integral to this diffusion arc, most extant research examined DTC-GT news coverage predating the 2017 spike in DTC-GT kit popularity (Regalado, 2019), perhaps missing a crucial shift in the interaction between DTC-GT and consumers (e.g., Caburnay et al., 2014;Molina, 2004;Petersen, 2001). If "a frame makes reference to something resident in the surrounding culture" (Tewksbury & Scheufele, 2019, p. 23), then this swell in DTC-GT is a necessary cultural context in which to study DTC-GT framing. Missing in the literature is a systematic analysis of the prominence and dynamics of framing of DTC-GT over time and its potential association with real-world consumer behaviors.

Further, although the abundance of prior research lays the foundation for understanding DTC-GT framing, its reliance on deductive, predetermined and theoretically driven codebooks may have limited and biased findings (Matthes & Kohring, 2008), and would therefore benefit from a complementary inductive-deductive approaches to framing (van Gorp, 2010), especially in such a rapidly evolving context. Such a problem may be exacerbated by dynamic topics, where public concerns may change over time, as was the case with genetics. Our first research goal, therefore, is to offer a systematic analysis of news framing of DTC-GT over a decadelong period that captured the arc of DTC-GT popularity. Harnessing the power of inductive, computational frame identification and precision of a deductive, theory-driven qualitative analysis for its interpretation, we asked: RQ1: What were the frames used by news media covering DTC-GT kits? RQ2: How did the salience of frames change between 2009 and 2022?

Existing literature suggests that journalism on emerging technologies has an agenda that develops alongside the product. Since members of the public learn about emerging technologies through the media, this could also mirror a development in relevant public beliefs and attitudes. For example, as nanotechnology permeated society in the early 2000s, news coverage that once favored optimism of nanotechnology later highlighted conflict and regulatory concerns (Weaver et al., 2009). That is, as nanotechnology diffused into society, so too did related national discourse and public policy. Nanotechnology, like other high-potential yet high-risk technologies require developmental freedom to reach the "high potential" stage, but regulation tends to follow this stage, as their risks become more apparent (Mandel, 2009). This phenomenon has been found in several other contexts. For example, Chuan et al. (2019) found that, as AI innovations became more prominent in everyday life (high-potential stage), news coverage of AI became more attentive to AI policies, ethical concerns, and threats (high-risk stage). Early news coverage of DTC-GT depicts the high potentiality of DTC-GT, exemplifying stories of unambiguous scientific discovery and hope (Hellsten & Nerlich, 2008;Marks et al., 2007;Petersen, 2001). If the coverage and framing of DTC-GT follows the same pattern as other emerging technologies, it will have evolved to include more discussions of the potential risks associated with DTC-GT. Simply put, as the public becomes more familiar with emerging technologies, they also become more aware of the technologies' risks. Building on prior innovation coverage trends, and given that everyday people increasingly use DTC-GT, we hypothesize that: H1: The (a) volume of ethical concerns in and (b) negativity of news coverage will increase over time.

Correspondingly, trends can vary by the frames that constitute the topic model network. Although news stories do not lend themselves to setting up mathematically equivalent frames (Shah et al., 2009), their qualitative differences may be contextualized by the journalist's tone toward DTC-GT (e.g., promotional vs. critical). To gain a clearer understanding of the mechanics of over-time trends, we ask: RQ3: Did valence of coverage vary by media frame salience?

Given the complicated dynamics between emerging technology and social institutions, we might expect that news coverage influences not only how an individual perceives an emerging technology but also their behaviors relative to that technology. Yet a prominent criticism of emphasis framing research is that it is often too descriptive, neglecting the relationships between framing and audiences' behaviors (Borah, 2011). As such, it is important that we begin to explore the role that news media coverage might have on consumer behavior (e.g., buying DTC-GT). On one hand, consumer attitudes and behavior may be influenced by news coverage of biotechnology advancements and flaws (McCluskey et al., 2016), and landmark events (Ricci & Banterle, 2020). For example, reporting on scientific breakthroughs may increase the public's curiosity of DTC-GT technology and ultimately lead to increased DTC-GT kit sales. On the other hand, increases in consumer behavior could lead to increased attention from journalists, and even change the focus of journalistic coverage. This alternative plays into journalists' well-studied selectivity bias, by which news media tend to cover well-performing companies more frequently than low-performing companies (Nokelainen & Kanniainen, 2018). In this alternative scenario, consumer purchases drive the increase in news coverage. Both scenarios seem equally plausible and would speak to the influence of news framing on consumer behavior, but they have not yet been systematically examined. Given the lack of prior systematic research parsing the relationship between coverage and consumer behavior around DTC-GT, we ask: RQ4: What is the relationship, if any, between DTC-GT kit sales and the salience of specific DTC-GT media frames?

sections.paper_id: Dobmeier et al. - 2024 - Mapping the Media Genome An Unsupervised Machine Learning Analysis of News Framing of Direct-To-Con
Dobmeier et al. - 2024 - Mapping the Media Genome An Unsupervised Machine Learning Analysis of News Framing of Direct-To-Con
paragraphs: Using the news database Factiva, we collected a total of 5,151 unique news articles from local and national U.S. newspaper outlets between September 1, 2009 and September 1, 2022. The leading national outlets produced the highest number of articles, led by the New York Times (n = 722), followed by USA Today (465), the Washington Post (464), and the Wall Street Journal (426; see online supplemental materials). Newswires were excluded, along with newspapers that were not in English or based in the United States. The timespan aimed to capture changes over time, including during the height of popularity in the late 2010s. Articles were selected using the search terms "23andMe," "Ancestry.com," "AncestryDNA," and "MyHeritage." We focus on these DTC-GT companies due to their popularity among consumers, and to better parse the relationship between coverage and consumer purchasing behaviors (as opposed to including more general search terms like "genetic testing"). Interestingly, two of the leading companies were established during the time analyzed: AncestryDNA in 2012, and MyHeritage in 2016.

In pre-processing the corpus, we shifted all characters to lowercase, removed symbols and numbers, and removed common everyday words ("like," "for," etc.), artifacts from the text format of online news articles ("LP," "TD," "px"), and separators. Additionally, we trimmed words that appeared in the corpus too commonly (in more than 99% of articles) or rarely (in less than 2.5% of articles).

paragraphs: Consumer transaction data were obtained with permission from Bloomberg Second Measure (https://secondmeasure.com), a United States-based technology company that analyzes billions of anonymized purchases from a panel of 4.5 million Americans. This supplementary dataset reflects dayto-day purchase behaviors from January 1, 2012, to June 18, 2020, for each of 23andMe, AncestryDNA, and MyHeritage. It contains 3,092 observations for each company for a total of 9,276 observations. Similar datasets have previously been used to develop financial forecasting models (Fleder, 2019) and to replicate extant marketing research (McCarthy & Winer, 2019). We use this dataset to further explicate the interplay between media and consumer.

sections.paper_id: Dobmeier et al. - 2024 - Mapping the Media Genome An Unsupervised Machine Learning Analysis of News Framing of Direct-To-Con
paragraphs: This study uses a novel inductive-first approach, the Analysis of Topic Model Networks (ANTMN; Walter & Ophir, 2019), to analyze all coverage of genetic testing published in national and local U.S. outlets between September 2009 and September 2022. ANTMN's strength lies in its mixedmethods nature, which gives it the ability to analyze big data corpora, using a four-stage approach that combines three synergistic computational methods-topic modeling, network analysis, and community detection-and a deductive, qualitative analysis (Walter & Ophir, 2019). The inductive nature of this unsupervised machine learning method is an important complement to prior deductive DTC-GT content analyses, such that it may (a) validate frames found in prior work (e.g., Lynch et al., 2011), (b) uncover novel frames from similar corpora (Ophir et al., 2023), and (c) account for the dynamic nature of frames over time, especially for corpora in which the set of possible topics and themes is not known a-priori (Maier et al., 2018). The first three steps employ an inductive, data-driven approach, while the fourth allows for a deductive, theory-driven supplemental analysis (Walter et al., 2022).

In the first step, an unsupervised topic modeling method (Latent Dirichlet Allocation [LDA] in this paper), clusters large textual data into topics from a distribution of word probabilities based on the cooccurrences of words within the same documents (Blei, 2012) into topics. A Gibbs sampling, 5-fold cross-validation algorithm optimized our model at an alpha-value of 0.1 and number of topics k = 34. For each topic, ANTMN provides the 50 most representative words, the 30 most representative news articles, and the 50 most representative unique words using the frequency-exclusivity method (FREX; M. E. Roberts et al., 2014). FREX words-keywords that appear frequently throughout a frame element but infrequently in others-may help to distinguish similar frame elements. Each topic was then manually labeled based on a close reading of its 30 exemplary news articles and, if necessary, the FREX words. We discarded one topic with no coherent thematic meaning (Walter & Ophir, 2019), resulting in a model of 33 topics.

In the second step, ANTMN calculates a network of those topics, based on their co-occurrences within news articles (using cosine similarity over the document-topic matrix; Blei, 2012). The similarities are then used as network edges and each topic serves as a node. Again, the inductive nature of the network analysis pushes researchers to scrutinize the discursive similarities across topics that may have otherwise been deemed by human coders too dissimilar to compare (Matthes & Kohring, 2008). Importantly, ANTMN does not assume that individual topics constitute frames, but instead treats topics as frame elements that could be clustered into frames in the next step (Walter & Ophir, 2019).

In the third step, a community detection algorithm is applied to the network model to identify clusters of nodes (topics), or what scholars often relate to as frames (Entman, 1993). In other words, the topics are operationalized as frame elements, and the clusters of topics as frame packages (Entman, 1993;van Gorp, 2010). The inductive quality of the automated cluster analysis gives way to the emergence of frames that may not have been empirically determined in prior research (Matthes & Kohring, 2008). Using the leading eigenvector community detection algorithm, which is suitable for the large number of frame elements in our model (Mothe et al., 2017), we find four distinct frames. By the end of this computational sequence, each news article in the corpus is defined by a combination of all four frames identified in the model-not by any single frame element.

Finally, the fourth stage includes an in-depth, deductive manual analysis by experts, in our case in the field of science and health communication. Each eigenvector community detected in the ANTMN model (i.e., frame) was labeled upon consideration of all its constituent frame elements and its ten most representative news articles. Frames identified in extant DTC-GT research were consulted to make sense of those identified in our model. This manual analysis supplements the broader view obtained from the unsupervised machine learning approach with additional theory-driven analyses.

Using the ANTMN model, we examined the change of frames over time and its relationship with sales data, using vector autoregressive models (VAR) to control for linear trends in the variables. We used an ADF test to determine the optimal number of lags for the VAR models. To supplement the ANTMN analyses, we conducted a manual content analysis on a random subsample of approximately 10% of news articles in the total corpus (n = 500), coding for valence and a range of ethical issues. To build reliability, two independent coders trained on a random subsample of 100 news articles in a preliminary dataset. 1 To accommodate for the nonnormal distributions of ethical issues within articles (see Quarfoot & Levine, 2016), interrater reliability was calculated using the Brennan-Prediger (BP) coefficient (Brennan & Prediger, 1981). A codebook containing descriptions of and reliability scores for each item can be found in Table 2 in the online supplemental materials.

sections.paper_id: Dobmeier et al. - 2024 - Mapping the Media Genome An Unsupervised Machine Learning Analysis of News Framing of Direct-To-Con
paragraphs: Valence and ethical concerns were found in prior research to play a key role in the introduction and discussion of new technologies (e.g., Lynch et al., 2011). However, news frames are not inherently positive or negative; for example, a public order frame found in social protest coverage included discussions of both violent (negative) and peaceful (positive) protests (Ophir et al., 2023). We coded valence specifically for the statements made about DTC-GT (BP = .87), rather than for the entire article, on a scale from (1) negative/pessimistic to (5) positive/optimistic. Articles perceived to be either balanced in negative and positive statements, or absent of DTC-GT valence statements, were coded as (3) neutral. For example, an article received a score of 1 if its DTC-GT kit attitude statements were overwhelmingly negative, like one from the Pittsburgh Post-Gazette (2018), which discussed the "DNA-testing folly" and its implications, explicitly stating "that the potential dangers outweigh the novelty" (n.p.). Conversely, an article received a 5 if its relevant attitude statements painted an overwhelmingly optimistic picture of the technology without mentioning its implications, like an article from the USA Today, which listed "the top companies offering genealogical DNA testing to learn about your family" (Weise, 2018).

sections.paper_id: Dobmeier et al. - 2024 - Mapping the Media Genome An Unsupervised Machine Learning Analysis of News Framing of Direct-To-Con
paragraphs: Ethical issues listed by the NHGRI (2021) were coded for (0) absence or (1) presence and covered a span of issues: [insurance] coverage and reimbursement of tests, genetic discrimination, health disparities, human subjects research, informed consent, intellectual property, privacy, regulation of tests, and synthetic biology (BPs ranging from .87 to 1.00; see online supplemental materials). The most dominant ethical issues in DTC-GT coverage regarded regulation (18.7%) and privacy (11.7%).

sections.paper_id: Dobmeier et al. - 2024 - Mapping the Media Genome An Unsupervised Machine Learning Analysis of News Framing of Direct-To-Con
paragraphs: RQ1 asked what frames were used by news media covering DTC-GT kits. Our topic model sorted the corpus into 33 frame elements (nodes), clustered into four coherent frames: utility (13 topics; 38.24% of the total corpus), political-economic (9; 26.47%), scientific (6; 17.65%), and commercial (6; 17.65%). Figure 1 presents the topics network (refer to the online supplemental materials for the frame elements associated with each frame).

The utility frame was the most salient frame in our model. It primarily spotlighted the socio-cultural utility of DTC-GT kits. For example, using this frame, the media explored the general applications of DTC-GT kits in frame elements, particularly as they pertain to finding family, archiving genealogy, and advertising community events and workshops that help the public get involved in tracing their ancestry. Some frame elements focused on the socio-cultural history of the United States, and how DTC-GT could shed light on consumers' relations to the American history (e.g., the frame elements "race and slavery," and "historical figures").

Next, articles exemplifying the political-economic frame contained language relating to politics and politicians (e.g., "politicians"), the economy (e.g., "stock market"), and big business (e.g., "billionaires" and "budgets"). This frame captures the influential role of DTC-GT companies and their CEOs in popular business discourse, and how such influence has drawn investors toward these companies and the broader genetic testing industry.

The scientific frame regards clinical and genomics research, placing DTC-GT kits in the heart of conversations on technological and medical innovation (e.g., "scientific awards," "research studies," "drugs"), as well as counterpart ethical concerns (e.g., "crime solving" and "data and privacy"). Many of these articles reference major news events, such as 23andMe's regulatory battle with the FDA, the use of DTC-GT data to find and prosecute the Golden State Killer, and the breach of MyHeritage user data.

Contrary to the abovementioned frames, which focused on the social and scientific applications of DTC-GT, the fourth frame identified kits as products, through a commercial lens. Articles using the commercial frame offered practical advice on how to choose and encouragement to purchase a DTC-GT kit. Often in such coverage, "editors review and recommend products to help you buy the stuff you need" (from our corpus: Gordon, 2018). Interestingly, these articles frequently stated that the editor or outlet "may earn a small share of the revenue" from sales due to these editorial advertisements, or "advertorials."

RQ2 asked how the relative salience of frames changed over time. Generally, as coverage increased over time (an increase that exhibited a linear trend that was statistically significant, as evident regressing volume on year, b = 27.33, SE = 9.42, p = .013), news media maintained a strong emphasis on DTC-GT kits' utility, such as discussing one's ability to discover their ethnicity. However, over the full timespan, utility exhibited a linear trend of decline (b = -.02, SE = .00, p < .001) The news media also frequently talked about the scientific aspects of DTC-GT kits, such as their medical viability. Typically, an increase in scientific framing came at the expense of utility framing, and vice versa (see Figure 2). Conversely, political-economic framing of DTC-GT kits, such as emphasizing government regulation, often coincided with an increase in scientific framing, though not always (e.g., May to July, 2016). Finally, commercial framing exhibited seasonal upticks, but also a steady increase throughout the decade (b = .01, SE = .00, p < .001). For example, in 2018, DTC-GT articles with such framing nearly doubled around Black Friday (November 23) from its average count in preceding months. all variables were stationary) with Granger causality. The analysis indicated a nuanced relationship between genetic testing kits' sales and the amount of coverage focused on commercialism (F = 48.28, p < .001, with a lag of one week, selected based on the Augmented Dickey-Fuller test). We found that increases in sales were associated with an increase of commercialism coverage after one week (B = .00, SE = .00, p = .022) and a decrease after two weeks (B = -.00, SE = .00, p = .004). The Granger causality test suggested that sales influenced coverage, and not vice versa. The test also rejected instantaneous effects. No significant relationships were found between sales and any of the other frames (p > .05).

This frame captures the influential role of DTC-GT companies and their CEOs in popular business discourse, and how such influence has drawn investors toward these companies and the broader genetic testing industry.

The scientific frame regards clinical and genomics research, placing DTC-GT kits in the heart of conversations on technological and medical innovation (e.g., "scientific awards," "research studies," "drugs"), as well as counterpart ethical concerns (e.g., "crime solving" and "data and privacy"). Many of these articles reference major news events, such as 23andMe's regulatory battle with the FDA, the use of DTC-GT data to find and prosecute the Golden State Killer, and the breach of MyHeritage user data.

Contrary to the abovementioned frames, which focused on the social and scientific applications of DTC-GT, the fourth frame identified kits as products, through a commercial lens. Articles using the commercial frame offered practical advice on how to choose and encouragement to purchase a DTC-GT kit. Often in such coverage, "editors review and recommend products to help you buy the stuff you need" (from our corpus: Gordon, 2018). Interestingly, these articles frequently stated that the editor or outlet "may earn a small share of the revenue" from sales due to these editorial advertisements, or "advertorials."

RQ2 asked how the relative salience of frames changed over time. Generally, as coverage increased over time (an increase that exhibited a linear trend that was statistically significant, as evident regressing volume on year, b = 27.33, SE = 9.42, p = .013), news media maintained a strong emphasis on DTC-GT kits' utility, such as discussing one's ability to discover their ethnicity. However, over the full timespan, utility exhibited a linear trend of decline (b = -.02, SE = .00, p < .001) The news media also frequently talked about the scientific aspects of DTC-GT kits, such as their medical viability. Typically, an increase in scientific framing came at the expense of utility framing, and vice versa (see Figure 2). Conversely, political-economic framing of DTC-GT kits, such as emphasizing government regulation, often coincided with an increase in scientific framing, though not always (e.g., May to July, 2016). Finally, commercial framing exhibited seasonal upticks, but also a steady increase throughout the decade (b = .01, SE = .00, p < .001). For example, in 2018, DTC-GT articles with such framing nearly doubled around Black Friday (November 23) from its average count in preceding months. all variables were stationary) with Granger causality. The analysis indicated a nuanced relationship between genetic testing kits' sales and the amount of coverage focused on commercialism (F = 48.28, p < .001, with a lag of one week, selected based on the Augmented Dickey-Fuller test). We found that increases in sales were associated with an increase of commercialism coverage after one week (B = .00, SE = .00, p = .022) and a decrease after two weeks (B = -.00, SE = .00, p = .004). The Granger causality test suggested that sales influenced coverage, and not vice versa. The test also rejected instantaneous effects. No significant relationships were found between sales and any of the other frames (p > .05).

paragraphs: Our computational analysis of 13 years of media coverage of DTC-GT identified the use of distinct and coherent frames: utility, scientific, and political-economic. In addition, part of the corpus was dedicated to a commercial frame that treated DTC-GT as consumer products. The prominence of the utility frame is congruent with prior work on biotechnologies in general (Listerman, 2010) and DTC-GT kits specifically (Lynch et al., 2011). Utility framing in our corpus concerned mostly social uses of DTC-GT, such as learning about one's ancestry through DNA testing (for example, McWhirter, 2016). The scientific frame is congruent with frames found in other scientific contexts, such as stem cells (Nisbet et al., 2003). In the context of DTC-GT, scientific framing was used most to discuss scientific studies that advance the field of genomics, such as a study leveraging 23andMe data to find genetic links to depression (see Cha, 2016). Similarly, our political-economic frame is congruent with Nisbet et al. (2003) discussion of CEOs, venture capitalism, and stock markets. In the case of DTC-GT kits, articles often centered around Silicon Valley and the DTC-GT company CEOs that live there, such as an article on a day in the life of 23andMe CEO Anne Wojcicki (Carter, 2016).

In addition to these frames, which validate other work on the coverage of science and technology, our analysis identified a substantial share of topics portraying kits as consumer products. A commercial frame has not been identified in prior research and is largely ignored in most work on the framing of science. Articles using such framing were often explicit about the endorsement of products. For example, an article in our corpus by Gordon (2018) claimed that:

Right now, you can find out if your family really did have origins in the Roman Empire or if you've got a long-lost relative. Ancestry's DNA kits are on sale on Amazon for 30% off right now, just a little bit less than the low price we saw recently during the DNA Day sale. (para. 4) This example illustrates that the technology and the science around it was often tied to a consumer product and explicitly prompted individuals to buy said product. This relationship has practical implications as it suggests that individuals might view the test kits as a consumer product rather than as an important, complicated tool meant to provide insight into their health and wellbeing. As such, future research should investigate how this association might influence perceptions of emerging technologies as both scientific and consumer products.

Although prior newspaper content analyses on DTC-GT kits have focused largely or exclusively on substantive coverage (e.g., Caburnay et al., 2014;Caulfield et al., 2008;Lynch et al., 2011), our results demonstrate that promotional coverage of DTC-GT kits captures a noteworthy 11.78% of all news coverage. In part, this finding could be understood in light of institutional changes, where news agencies' reliance on ad revenue and cooperation with public relations and advertisers have increased in the competitive Internet era (Angelucci & Cagé, 2019). We believe that these articles, while not fitting traditional definitions of emphasis frames (Entman, 1993), should still be considered part of the information landscape around technology and scientific products, as they can influence audiences' opinions and behaviors, for example by increasing readers' issue and product familiarity (e.g., E. W. Lee & Ho, 2015). Further, as McCluskey and Swinnen (2004) note, obvious financial incentives, as demonstrated by the commercial frame, may hint at a news outlet's predisposition to positively bias coverage of certain products. While news content tends to bias toward negativity (Soroka & McAdams, 2015), it is the positive framing of products, evident in these topics in our corpus, that could increase consumer interest and trust in the products (H. C. Lee et al., 2018). A content analysis by Einsiedel and Geransar (2009) noted the importance of the ways DTC-GT companies positively frame their own products, but it is plausible that promotional news coverage in outlets across the country would have a stronger effect on audiences. Seeing as promotional news coverage has largely been overlooked when considering the framing of science in general and DTC-GT in particular, uncovering such potential influence gives credence to our inductive-first approach.
These results indicate that it could be a fruitful area for future mass communication research.

Our second research question asked how the aforementioned frames in DTC-GT kit coverage changed between 2009 and 2022. As expected, DTC-GT kit coverage increased dramatically over the decade in terms of total volume as kits made their way into mainstream culture, and their applications diversified; for example, DTC-GT kits' health application expanded from cancer risk detection to precision medicine. As such, the utility frame remained the dominant frame almost entirely throughout the data collection period, suggesting that as kits can become used in more ways, there is more that journalists can write about them. However, in the peak of DTC-GT kit coverage (November 2018), commercial framing of kits (over 40% frame salience across articles) exceeded utility framing of them (about 25% salience; Figure 2), alluding to a swell in consumer-centric discourse around frame elements "Advertising," "Deals," "Sales," and especially "Black Friday sales."foot_3 This trend of promotional news coverage has not held prominence in framing research, but should be considered in the greater scope of science communication: the adoption of promotional coverage may come at the expense of journalists' role as public advocates.

While substantive coverage remained relatively stable over the decade, promotional coverage only emerged in later news. Particularly, commercial framing remained scarce (below 10% across coverage) until November 2017, corresponding with both Black Friday and peak kit sales (Figure 2), during which DTC-GT kits were promoted as "one of the hottest items for Black Friday 2017" (Campbell, 2018). Similarly behaved trends can be seen in later years, during which newspaper editors more frequently recommended these increasingly popular kits as gifts. Unsurprisingly, these seasonal trends repeated throughout the rest of our corpus: overall coverage and commercial framing had large upticks each subsequent Black Friday (40% salience in 2018, 42% in 2019, 58% in 2020, and 54% in 2021). Given these trends, it becomes clear that, at least after the 2017 spike in DTC-GT popularity, holiday sales were factored into the news coverage calculus.

Next, we looked at prevalence of ethical issues within (H1a) and valence of (H1b) DTC-GT kit coverage over time. From 2009 to 2022, we find a marginally significant linear trend in ethical discourse and no significant change in valence across all frames over time, though we do find significant valence trends by frames, with utility and commercial framing covering DTC-GT kits in a positive light, and political-economic and scientific framing covering the kits in a negative light (RQ3). These nonlinear findings illustrate the importance of taking a nuanced approach to studying patterns over time. These findings also highlight how critical it is to consider how consumerism could be tied to the object of study and how journalists correspondingly position them as products. As such, future research should examine the comparative influence of substantive framing and promotional framing. Because topic modeling is a mixed-family approach, where each text is operationalized to be a mixture of all available frames, we cannot run separate analyses for substantive and promotional coverage. Future studies may look further into these questions.

Our analysis of the relationship between DTC-GT kits sales and news framing (RQ4) showed that the media partially responded to changes in real-world sales. Specifically, as sales of kits increased, so did the media's focus on commercial framing (with a lag of one week), though coverage decreased in the week after. In other words, when DTC-GT kits sales increased, news media reacted by emphasizing commercialism for a short time, but the salience of these discussions waned quickly after. Further, there was no relationship between sales and the use of the scientific, utility or political-economic frames. Conversely, we found no indication that media coverage influenced DTC-GT kit sales. Importantly, like other tests based on Granger causality, we recommend caution in interpreting the causal direction of the relationship. In light of these findings, we side with other researchers in encouraging more research into the effects of media frames on real-world behaviors (Borah, 2011).

Lastly, future research should leverage various research methods to expand beyond correlational claims. As we consider balanced coverage that discusses both benefits and risks of DTC-GT usage, we acknowledge that the question of whether more negative or positive coverage should be considered more or less ethical on the journalists' side is open for debate and is inherently subjective. We do not expect journalists or consumers to hold a unified view of the advantages and disadvantages of this innovation. Since the news media may commercialize products in ways eschewing their ethicality or detriments for individuals or society at large, it is important to pinpoint the psychological-not just communicative-mechanisms driving both journalistic coverage and consumer behavior, and the roles played by valence and ethicality, which cannot be fully explored by the methods used in the current study. For example, interviews and ethnographies might best explore explicit journalistic motivations of framing or normative consumer tendencies related to DTC-GT products and services, while experimental designs might unearth psychological factors in consumers' calculus of DTC-GT risks and benefits. Further, as news media coverage on genetic risk leads to increased interpersonal discussions on it (Cappella et al., 2007), future research should also look into the interpersonal conversations around DTC-GT kits and their potential to facilitate, amplify, or dampen mass media messages (Southwell & Yzer, 2007).

In sum, this study used an inductive-first approach to systematically identify the frames through which the media cover direct-to-consumer genetic testing kits, analyze changes in framing over a decade, and examine the interplay between the media's coverage and the consumer purchasing of these kits. Despite potential risks incurred by their use, direct-to-consumer genetic testing kits have gained extraordinary popularity, with over 15% of U.S. adults having used one by 2019 (Pew Research Center, 2019). Understanding the ways in which the media cover such innovations and its potential impact on audiences could guide future science communications around the benefits and risks of other biotechnologies like vaccines, GMOs, and CRISPR. Our results point to a gap in research on coverage of technology and scientific innovation, suggesting a need to better consider the ways news outlets conceive technology through the lens of consumerism and its potential theoretical and practical implications.

In addition, part of the corpus was dedicated to a commercial frame that treated DTC-GT as consumer products. The prominence of the utility frame is congruent with prior work on biotechnologies in general (Listerman, 2010) and DTC-GT kits specifically (Lynch et al., 2011).
sections.preview_tail: Despite potential risks incurred by their use, direct-to-consumer genetic testing kits have gained extraordinary popularity, with over 15% of U.S. adults having used one by 2019 (Pew Research Center, 2019). Understanding the ways in which the media cover such innovations and its potential impact on audiences could guide future science communications around the benefits and risks of other biotechnologies like vaccines, GMOs, and CRISPR. Our results point to a gap in research on coverage of technology and scientific innovation, suggesting a need to better consider the ways news outlets conceive technology through the lens of consumerism and its potential theoretical and practical implications.
